topics.title: Chemistry of Food Allergens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT634 - FOOD TOXICOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chemistry of Food Allergens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Allergen (KTU 2024 Definition):** A food allergen is a normally harmless, immunologically active macromolecule — almost always a **glycoprotein** with a molecular weight typically in the range of **10–70 kDa** — that, in susceptible individuals, triggers a Type I (IgE-mediated) hypersensitivity reaction as classified by Coombs and Gell.

The chemistry of food allergens therefore deals with the **molecular structure, physicochemical stability, epitope configuration, and biochemical reactivity** of these antigenic proteins and glycoproteins that survive food processing and gastrointestinal digestion to provoke an immune response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Allergen** = the specific molecular entity (e.g., Ara h 1 from peanut).
> - **Allergenic Food** = the whole food source (e.g., peanut).
> - **Allergenicity** = the intrinsic ability of a molecule to elicit IgE.
> - **Antigenicity** = the ability to bind antibodies (broader term).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lock and Key Trap"

Imagine a hotel with millions of rooms (your body's proteins). Normally, only the **"Master Key"** (immune system IgE antibodies) opens specific rooms. A food allergen is like a **fake key deliberately crafted to look exactly like the master key**. It fools the body's security system, triggering alarms (histamine release, anaphylaxis) even though the "intruder" (allergen) is actually harmless food protein.

**Breakdown of the analogy:**
- **Fake Key (Allergen)** → Surface-shaped glycoprotein mimicking antigen
- **Door Lock (IgE on Mast Cells)** → Pre-formed antibodies sitting on immune cells
- **Alarm Bell (Histamine Release)** → Symptoms: hives, swelling, breathing difficulty
- **Key's Unique Teeth (Epitope)** → 15–22 amino acid region that binds IgE

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Big-8" / "Big-9" Allergens (FDA & FALCPA, USA; mandatory KTU knowledge):**
> 1. Milk, 2. Eggs, 3. Fish, 4. Shellfish, 5. Tree nuts, 6. Peanuts, 7. Wheat, 8. Soybeans (+ **Sesame** added in 2023, making it the Big-9).
>
> These account for **>90%** of all food-allergic reactions globally.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Property | Typical Value for Food Allergens |
|---|---|
| Molecular weight | **10–70 kDa** |
| Isoelectric point (pI) | Acidic (4–6) or Basic (8–10) |
| Glycosylation | Often present (10–40% carbohydrate) |
| Thermal stability | High — survives boiling, baking |
| Protease resistance | High — survives pepsin (stomach) and trypsin (intestine) |
| Epitope size | **5–24 amino acids** (B-cell); 12–20 (T-cell) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Biochemical Classification of Food Allergen Protein Families

Food allergens are not random — over **70%** belong to a small number of structurally related protein families. This explains **cross-reactivity** (when the body reacts to multiple foods).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield: The 4 Major Plant Allergen Protein Families**

| Protein Family | Members | Typical MW | Example Allergen | Special Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Prolamin Superfamily** | 2S albumins, nsLTP, α-amylase inhibitors | **12–16 kDa** | Ara h 2 (peanut), Tri a 19 (wheat ω-gliadin) | Compact disulfide bonds → heat/pH resistant |
| **Cupin Superfamily** | 7S (vicilin) & 11S (legumin) globulins | **40–80 kDa** | Ara h 1 (peanut 7S), Ara h 3 (peanut 11S) | Trimeric/hexameric, high stability |
| **Profilins** | Cytoskeletal actin binders | **12–15 kDa** | Bet v 2 (birch), Pru av 4 (cherry) | Pan-allergen — causes cross-reactivity |
| **PR-10 Proteins (Bet v 1 homologues)** | Pathogenesis-related | **16–18 kDa** | Mal d 1 (apple), Cor a 1 (hazelnut) | Heat-labile, labile to digestion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Animal Allergen Protein Families (KTU Favourite):**
> - **Caseins** (milk): α-, β-, κ-casein — calcium-binding phosphoproteins, **~19–25 kDa**.
> - **Whey proteins** (milk): β-lactoglobulin (**Bos d 5**, 18.3 kDa), α-lactalbumin (Bos d 4, 14.2 kDa).
> - **Tropomyosins** (shellfish/fish/mites): **~33–38 kDa**, coiled-coil, pan-allergen.
> - **Parvalbumins** (fish): **~12 kDa**, calcium-binding — major pan-allergen across fish species.

---

### 2.2 Allergen Nomenclature — WHO/IUIS System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Format: Genus species + number**
> Example: **Ara h 1** → *Arachis hypogaea* allergen number 1
>
> - First 3 letters = genus
> - Next letter = species
> - Number = chronological order of identification

---

### 2.3 Epitopes — The Molecular "Teeth of the Key"

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Types of Epitopes (KTU 2024 Highlight):**
> 1. **Conformational (Discontinuous) Epitopes:** Made of amino acids brought close by the 3D folding of the protein. Destroyed by **denaturation** (heat, pH). Example: many IgE epitopes on β-lactoglobulin.
> 2. **Linear (Continuous) Epitopes:** Short stretches of **5–24 amino acids** in primary sequence. Resistant to denaturation. Example: casein epitopes survive baking.

**Why does this matter for food processing?**
- **Baking peanuts** → retains allergenicity (linear epitopes intact in Ara h 2).
- **Cooking apples** → often tolerated by birch-allergic patients (PR-10 conformational epitopes destroyed).

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Threshold Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Although food allergen chemistry is qualitative, the following equations and constants govern analysis:**

**1. Molecular Weight from SDS-PAGE:**
$$MW = (\text{Relative Migration Distance}) \times (\text{Calibration Slope}) + \text{Intercept}$$

**2. Allergen Stability Index (Ayers et al., modified):**
$$SI = \frac{IgE\text{ binding after processing}}{IgE\text{ binding of native allergen}} \times 100\%$$

**3. Threshold Dose (LOAEL approach — KTU Board Hot Topic):**
$$\text{ED}_{50} = \text{Dose at which } 50\% \text{ of the allergic population reacts}$$

**4. Optical Density for ELISA detection:**
$$\text{Concentration (ppm)} = \frac{OD_{sample} - OD_{blank}}{OD_{standard} - OD_{blank}} \times C_{standard}$$

**5. Protein Digestibility Index (for allergenicity risk):**
$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\text{Amino acids released by pepsin}}{\text{Total amino acids}} \times 100$$

> **Standard Threshold:** If PDI < **50%** in 60 min of pepsin digestion → high allergenic risk (FAO/WHO Codex Alimentarius decision tree, 2001).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering/Industry Utility

| Application | Use of Allergen Chemistry |
|---|---|
| **Food Industry R&D** | Designing hypoallergenic variants (e.g., enzymatic hydrolysis of whey) |
| **Detection Kit Manufacturing** | ELISA & PCR-based kits rely on epitope knowledge |
| **Food Labelling Laws (FALCPA, FSSAI)** | Big-9 declaration requires chemical identification |
| **Biotechnology** | Recombinant hypo-allergens with mutated epitopes for immunotherapy |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Decision-Tree for Allergenicity Assessment (FAO/WHO Codex, 2001)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a KTU board favourite for 7-mark derivations.** The decision tree evaluates whether a novel protein is a potential allergen based on sequence homology, resistance to digestion, and serum reactivity.

**Step 1 — Establish Molecular Identity**
$$M_{protein} = M_{peptide\,fragments} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (M_{aa,i} - 18) + 18$$

Where:
- $M_{aa,i}$ = mass of each amino acid residue
- **18 g/mol** = mass of water lost per peptide bond
- $n$ = number of residues

> *Logic:* Mass-spectrometric identification of the intact protein must precede any allergenicity claim.

---

**Step 2 — Sequence Homology Comparison (BLAST/BLAT search)**
$$\% \text{ Identity} = \frac{\text{Number of identical aligned residues}}{\text{Length of shorter sequence}} \times 100$$

**Threshold criterion:**
$$\text{If } \%\text{Identity} > 35\% \text{ over a sliding window of } \geq 80 \text{ aa} \Rightarrow \text{POTENTIAL CROSS-REACTIVITY}$$

> *Logic:* Significant homology suggests shared epitopes — the body may recognize the new protein as the same allergen.

---

**Step 3 — Pepsin Resistance Assay**
The protein is exposed to **simulated gastric fluid (SGF)** with pepsin at **pH 1.2, 37 °C**.

$$t_{1/2}^{\text{fragmentation}} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{digestion}}$$

Where:
$$k_{digestion} = \frac{0.693}{t_{1/2}}$$

> *Logic:* A protein that persists intact for >60 min is classified as **persistent** — a key marker of allergenicity (Codex 2001 criterion).

---

**Step 4 — IgE Cross-Reactivity Test**
$$\text{Percent Inhibition} = \left(1 - \frac{OD_{sample\,+\,inhibitor}}{OD_{sample\,without\,inhibitor}}\right) \times 100$$

> *Logic:* If sera from known-allergic patients bind the novel protein, allergenicity is confirmed.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Stability Index Calculation

**Problem:** A batch of β-lactoglobulin (Bos d 5) is hydrolysed with trypsin to produce a "hypoallergenic" whey. ELISA shows:
- Native Bos d 5 IgE binding = **1.250 OD**
- Hydrolysed sample IgE binding = **0.075 OD**

**Find the Stability Index and comment on safety.**

**Solution:**

$$\text{Step 1: } OD_{corrected} = OD_{sample} - OD_{blank} = 0.075 - 0.020 = 0.055$$

$$\text{Step 2: } OD_{native,corrected} = 1.250 - 0.020 = 1.230$$

$$\text{Step 3: Apply SI formula: } SI = \frac{0.055}{1.230} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Step 4: } SI = 4.47\%$$

> **Conclusion:** With **SI = 4.47%** (well below 50% threshold), the hydrolysate is considered **hypoallergenic** and is suitable for hypoallergenic infant formulas.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Food Allergens

| Allergen Source | Key Allergen (WHO/IUIS) | MW (kDa) | Protein Family | Heat Stable? | Digestion Stable? | Cross-Reactivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cow's Milk | Bos d 5 (β-lactoglobulin) | 18.3 | Lipocalin | Yes | Yes | Goat, sheep milk |
| Hen's Egg | Gal d 1 (ovomucoid) | 28 | Kazal-type serine protease inhibitor | **Yes (very)** | **Yes (very)** | Other bird eggs |
| Peanut | Ara h 1, Ara h 2, Ara h 3 | 65, 17, 60 | 7S, 2S, 11S | Yes | Yes | Lupin, soy |
| Soybean | Gly m 5, Gly m 6 | 48, 53 | 7S, 11S | Moderate | Moderate | Peanut, lupin |
| Wheat | Tri a 19 (ω-5 gliadin) | 65 | Prolamin | Yes | Yes | Other cereals |
| Shrimp | Pen a 1 (tropomyosin) | 36 | Tropomyosin | **Yes** | **Yes** | Crab, lobster, mites, cockroach |
| Fish | Gad c 1 (parvalbumin) | 12 | EF-hand Ca²⁺ binder | Yes | Yes | Multiple fish species |
| Apple | Mal d 1 | 17 | PR-10 | **No** | **No** | Birch pollen |
| Sesame | Ses i 1, Ses i 2 | 9, 17 | 2S albumin, 7S | Yes | Yes | Mustard, hazelnut |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IgE-Mediated Hypersensitivity Cascade (Type I Reaction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most frequently drawn diagram in KTU exams.** It shows how allergen chemistry translates into clinical symptoms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Allergen Protein Ingestion] --> B[Antigen Presentation by APC to Th2 Cell]
    B --> C[Th2 Releases IL-4 and IL-13]
    C --> D[B Cell Class Switching to IgE]
    D --> E[IgE Binds FcεRI on Mast Cell]
    E --> F[Sensitization Phase Complete]
    F --> G[Re-exposure to Same Allergen]
    G --> H[Allergen Cross-links Two IgE on Mast Cell]
    H --> I[Degranulation: Histamine, Tryptase, LTC4, PAF]
    I --> J[Clinical Symptoms: Urticaria, Angioedema, Bronchospasm, Anaphylaxis]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Left branch (F)** → Asymptomatic sensitization.
- **Right branch (H → I → J)** → Symptoms appear only on re-exposure (memory of IgE).
- **Cross-linking** (H) is the *chemistry* — bivalent allergen must bridge two IgE molecules.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Allergen Classification & Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source[Source Material]
        S1[Milk]
        S2[Egg]
        S3[Peanut/Tree nut]
        S4[Shellfish/Fish]
        S5[Wheat/Soy]
    end

    Source --> Extract[Protein Extraction Buffer pH 8.0]

    Extract --> Fraction[Size Fractionation: 10 kDa to 100 kDa]

    Fraction --> Identify[Immunoblotting with Patient IgE Sera]

    Identify --> Classify[Classification by Protein Family]

    Classify --> P1[Prolamin Superfamily]
    Classify --> P2[Cupin Superfamily]
    Classify --> P3[Profilin/PR-10]
    Classify --> P4[Tropomyosin/Parvalbumin]
    Classify --> P5[Casein/Whey]

    P1 --> Nomen[WHO/IUIS Nomenclature: Genus species Number]
    P2 --> Nomen
    P3 --> Nomen
    P4 --> Nomen
    P5 --> Nomen

    Nomen --> ELISA[ELISA Detection and Quantification in Foods]
    Nomen --> PCR[PCR for DNA-based Detection]
    Nomen --> LCMS[LC-MS/MS for Peptide Confirmation]
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- The **left** side represents physical sample processing.
- The **middle** represents immunological identification.
- The **right** side represents the three industrial detection modalities (ELISA, PCR, LC-MS/MS), each exploiting a different chemical property.

---

### 4.3 Epitope Topology Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph NativeProtein[Native Allergen Protein 3D Folded]
        E1[Conformational Epitope A: Res 34, 56, 89, 120 brought close by folding]
        E2[Conformational Epitope B: Res 12, 67, 145 brought close by folding]
        L1[Linear Epitope 1: Res 55 to 65 continuous stretch]
        L2[Linear Epitope 2: Res 100 to 110 continuous stretch]
    end

    Heat[Heat Treatment / Denaturation] --> Denat[Unfolded Polypeptide Chain]

    E1 -.Destroyed.-> Denat
    E2 -.Destroyed.-> Denat
    L1 -.Survives.-> Denat
    L2 -.Survives.-> Denat
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- **Top:** Native folded allergen with mixed epitope types.
- **Bottom:** After heat, only linear epitopes survive — explains why baked milk/egg may still cause reactions (casein, ovomucoid) while some fruits lose allergenicity when cooked (Mal d 1).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

---

**Q1. Define the term "food allergen" as per WHO/IUIS nomenclature. Give one example with its proper designation.**

**Model Answer:**
A food allergen is a normally harmless dietary glycoprotein that elicits a Type I IgE-mediated hypersensitivity reaction in sensitized individuals. The WHO/IUIS system names allergens in the format: *Genus species* + number.

**Example:** Ara h 1 → *Arachis hypogaea* (peanut), allergen number 1, identified chronologically. It is a 7S vicilin (cupin superfamily) of 65 kDa, recognized as a major peanut allergen. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. List the "Big-8" food allergens. Why is sesame often added to make it the "Big-9"?**

**Model Answer:**

**Big-8:** Milk, Eggs, Fish, Shellfish, Tree nuts, Peanuts, Wheat, Soybeans.

**Reason for Big-9:** Sesame (*Sesamum indicum*) was added in April 2023 (US FDA) because of its rising prevalence (≈17% of pediatric food allergies in some regions), high stability (Ses i 1, a 2S albumin), and severity of reactions including anaphylaxis. India is now considering similar FSSAI amendments. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**(a) Classify food allergens based on their biochemical protein families. Discuss the prolamin superfamily in detail with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Food allergens are classified into four major plant and several animal protein families based on structural homology:

| Family | Plant Examples | Animal Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Prolamin superfamily | nsLTP, 2S albumins, α-amylase inhibitors | None (plant-specific) |
| Cupin superfamily | 7S vicilins, 11S legumins | None |
| Profilins | Bet v 2 | — |
| PR-10 / Bet v 1 homologues | Mal d 1, Cor a 1 | — |
| Tropomyosin | — | Pen a 1 (shrimp) |
| Parvalbumin | — | Gad c 1 (fish) |
| Caseins / Whey | — | Bos d 4, Bos d 5 (milk) |

**Prolamin Superfamily — Detailed Discussion (7 marks breakdown):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A superfamily of low-molecular-weight (12–16 kDa) proteins characterized by a conserved **cysteine-rich skeleton** (typically 6–8 Cys residues) forming 3–5 disulfide bonds.

- **Structural features (2 marks):**
  - 8 conserved cysteine residues forming disulfide bridges
  - Compact 3D fold → high resistance to heat, pH, and proteolysis
  - Often glycosylated

- **Key members (2 marks):**
  - **2S albumins:** Ara h 2 (peanut, 17 kDa), Ses i 1 (sesame, 9 kDa)
  - **Non-specific Lipid Transfer Proteins (nsLTP):** Pru p 3 (peach, 9 kDa)
  - **α-amylase inhibitors:** Hor v 15 (barley)

- **Clinical significance (2 marks):** Members show extensive **cross-reactivity** within the family. Their disulfide-bonded stability explains why peanut and sesame allergens persist through roasting, baking, and oil extraction.

---

**(b) Differentiate between conformational and linear epitopes. How does this knowledge influence food processing strategies? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Conformational Epitope | Linear Epitope |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Discontinuous residues brought together by 3D folding | Continuous stretch of 5–24 amino acids |
| Size | Variable; depends on folding | Typically 8–20 residues |
| Stability | Heat- and pH-labile | Heat- and pH-stable |
| Reduction by denaturation | Destroyed | Retained |
| Example | β-lactoglobulin (Bos d 5) | α- and β-casein, ovomucoid (Gal d 1) |

**Influence on food processing (7 marks):**

1. **Baked milk / baked egg oral immunotherapy (OIT):** Heating milk or egg destroys conformational epitopes on whey proteins and ovalbumin, but linear epitopes on **casein** and **ovomucoid** remain. However, patients desensitized to baked forms gradually tolerate the native forms. This is direct application of epitope chemistry. **(3 marks)**

2. **Apple processing (2 marks):** Mal d 1 (PR-10) is a conformational allergen — cooking apples destroys the epitope. Hence, patients with birch-fruit syndrome often tolerate apple sauce but not raw apple.

3. **Enzymatic hydrolysis (2 marks):** Trypsin + pepsin digests casein linear epitopes → hypoallergenic infant formulae (e.g., extensively hydrolysed casein — Nutramigen, Pregestimil).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**(a) Explain the WHO/FAO Codex Alimentarius decision tree for evaluating allergenicity of novel food proteins. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The FAO/WHO 2001 decision tree (updated 2017) is a stepwise evaluation:

**Step 1 — Source of the gene (1 mark):**
- Is it from a known allergenic source? If yes → assume allergenicity unless proven otherwise.

**Step 2 — Sequence homology (2 marks):**
- Compare with database of ≥ 200 known allergens.
- *Decision criterion:* >35% identity in a sliding window of 80 amino acids → cross-reactivity concern.

**Step 3 — Pepsin resistance test (2 marks):**
- Protein exposed to simulated gastric fluid (SGF) at pH 1.2.
- *Decision criterion:* Persistence of intact fragments after 60 min → high allergenic potential (PDI < 50%).

**Step 4 — Specific serum screening (1 mark):**
- Sera from allergic patients tested for IgE binding.

**Step 5 — Animal models & cell-based assays (1 mark):** Confirmatory.

The tree has been criticized for false positives (e.g., scorpion toxins pass the test) and refined in 2017 with additional targeted serum screening and epitope mapping.

---

**(b) Discuss in detail the chemistry of milk allergens and the molecular basis of cross-reactivity between cow's milk and goat's milk. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Chemistry of Milk Allergens (5 marks):**

| Allergen | Family | MW (kDa) | % of Milk Protein | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bos d 4 (α-lactalbumin) | Ca-binding | 14.2 | 4% | Heat-labile (conformational) |
| Bos d 5 (β-lactoglobulin) | Lipocalin | 18.3 | 10% | Heat-stable (linear epitopes) |
| Bos d 6 (BSA) | Serum albumin | 66 | 1% | Moderate |
| Bos d 7 (immunoglobulin) | IgG | 160 | 2% | Moderate |
| Bos d 8 (caseins: αs1, αs2, β, κ) | Casein phosphoprotein | 19–25 | 80% | **Highly heat-stable (linear)** |

Caseins are **phosphorylated, calcium-sensitive, micelle-forming proteins** with no tertiary structure → the molecular basis of their extreme thermal stability and allergenicity.

**Cross-reactivity between cow's and goat's milk (2 marks):**
- Casein sequences (Bos d 8) show **>87% identity** with goat caseins (Cap h 8).
- Hence, **>90%** of cow-milk-allergic patients also react to goat/sheep milk.
- However, **β-lactoglobulin differs more (~71%)**, so some patients tolerate goat whey but not goat casein.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "antigen" with "allergen"** (1 mark lost repeatedly). Remember: not every antigen is an allergen (allergenicity requires IgE induction).
> 2. **Writing "Ara h" instead of "Ara h 1"** — examiners deduct for incomplete nomenclature.
> 3. **Mixing up Bos d 5 (β-lactoglobulin) with Bos d 4 (α-lactalbumin)** — a classic trap in milk-allergen questions.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention that milk caseins are phosphoproteins** — this is a favourite 1-mark question.
> 5. **Drawing the hypersensitivity cascade without "cross-linking of IgE"** — the most critical chemical step. No cross-linking → no degranulation → no symptoms.
> 6. **Writing the threshold as "<50%" in the wrong direction** — high pepsin stability means *low* digestibility (<50%), not >50%.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Memory Sheet (Print This Before the Exam):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Food allergen = immunologically active glycoprotein (10–70 kDa) causing Type I hypersensitivity.
- ✅ **Big-9:** Milk, Egg, Fish, Shellfish, Tree nuts, Peanut, Wheat, Soy, Sesame.
- ✅ **Nomenclature:** Genus (3 letters) + species (1 letter) + number. Example: *Ara h 1*.
- ✅ **Plant families:** Prolamin, Cupin, Profilin, PR-10.
- ✅ **Animal families:** Tropomyosin (crustaceans), Parvalbumin (fish), Casein + Whey (milk).
- ✅ **Peanut major allergens:** Ara h 1 (7S), Ara h 2 (2S — most potent), Ara h 3 (11S).
- ✅ **Milk allergens:** Bos d 4 (α-lactalbumin), Bos d 5 (β-lactoglobulin), Bos d 8 (casein).
- ✅ **Egg allergens:** Gal d 1 (ovomucoid – heat stable), Gal d 2 (ovalbumin), Gal d 3 (ovotransferrin).
- ✅ **Epitope types:** Conformational (heat-labile) vs Linear (heat-stable).
- ✅ **Codex threshold:** >35% sequence identity over 80 aa = cross-reactivity risk.
- ✅ **Pepsin resistance:** Survives 60 min SGF = high allergenic potential.
- ✅ **Type I reaction key step:** Cross-linking of IgE on mast cells → degranulation → histamine.
- ✅ **Cross-reactivity examples:** Cow ↔ Goat milk, Shrimp ↔ Mites, Peanut ↔ Lupin, Apple ↔ Birch pollen.
- ✅ **Processing impact:** Baking destroys conformational; casein & ovomucoid retain linear epitopes; PR-10 allergens (Mal d 1) destroyed by cooking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Allergy and Hypersensitivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT634 - FOOD TOXICOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Food Allergy and Hypersensitivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Food hypersensitivity** is an umbrella term denoting objectively reproducible symptoms initiated by an exposure to a defined food stimulus at a dose tolerated by normal individuals. It is broadly classified into two clinical categories as established by the **European Academy of Allergy and Clinical Immunology (EAACI)** and adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus:

**Food Allergy** is a specific, reproducible adverse immune response to a food protein (allergen) mediated by the immunological system. It is dose-independent in sensitized individuals and involves immunoglobulin E (IgE), immunoglobulin G (IgG), T-cells, or immune complex mechanisms.

**Food Intolerance** is a non-immunological, reproducible adverse reaction resulting from metabolic disorders (e.g., lactase deficiency), pharmacological effects of food components (e.g., tyramine in cheese), or undefined mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Food allergy is strictly an **immune-mediated** response. Do not confuse it with food intolerance, which lacks immunological basis. Examiners specifically test this differentiation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Locked Door, Wrong Key" Concept

Imagine your immune system as a highly trained **security guard** standing at the door of your body. Under normal circumstances, the guard recognizes food proteins as harmless "residents" and lets them pass. 

In a food allergy:
- The guard has been **trained incorrectly** (sensitization phase) to view a harmless food protein like peanuts as a "dangerous intruder."
- When that protein (allergen) shows up, the guard fires the alarm by releasing **histamine, leukotrienes, and other inflammatory mediators** (elicitation phase).
- The body's "fire alarm" (allergic symptoms) goes off — manifesting as hives, swelling, vomiting, or in severe cases, **anaphylaxis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The dose of the allergen matters less than the body's erroneous immune recognition. Even a **trace amount (< 100 µg)** of peanut protein can trigger life-threatening reactions in a highly sensitized individual — this is why food labeling laws mandate disclosure even for minute quantities.

## 1.3 Standard Clinical Metrics

The following standard thresholds and epidemiological metrics are widely cited:

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Adult prevalence of food allergy (global) | **3% – 4%** |
| Pediatric prevalence of food allergy | **6% – 8%** |
| Threshold eliciting dose (ED) for peanut | **~ 0.1 mg protein** |
| Anaphylaxis incidence (food-induced) | **~ 30 cases per 100,000 person-years** |
| Reference dose (RfD, VITAL 3.0) for peanut | **0.2 mg** |

## 1.4 Coombs and Gell Classification (Foundational Framework)

The four classical types of hypersensitivity form the analytical backbone of food allergy:

- **Type I** — IgE-mediated (immediate, e.g., peanut anaphylaxis)
- **Type II** — Cytotoxic antibody-mediated (rare in food)
- **Type III** — Immune complex-mediated (rare in food)
- **Type IV** — Cell-mediated / delayed-type (e.g., celiac disease involves both Type IV and IgA mechanisms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Type I hypersensitivity is the **most frequently asked** hypersensitivity type in PEFTT634. Always describe both **sensitization** and **elicitation** phases in any 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type I IgE-Mediated Food Allergy — Operational Mechanism

The mechanism is divided into two chronological phases:

### Phase A: Sensitization (No Symptoms Yet)
1. **Initial exposure** — Food allergen (e.g., Ara h 1 from peanut) crosses the gastrointestinal mucosal barrier.
2. **Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs)** — Dendritic cells engulf the allergen, process it, and present peptide fragments on **MHC class II** molecules.
3. **T-helper 2 (Th2) polarization** — Naïve CD4⁺ T-cells differentiate into **Th2 cells** under the influence of cytokines **IL-4** and **IL-13**.
4. **B-cell class switching** — Th2 cells stimulate B-cells to undergo isotype switching from IgM to **allergen-specific IgE**.
5. **IgE binding** — Circulating IgE molecules bind with high affinity (**K$_d$ ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ M**) to **FcεRI receptors** on the surface of **mast cells** and **basophils**, "arming" them.

### Phase B: Elicitation (Symptoms Manifest)
1. **Re-exposure** — The same allergen binds and **cross-links** two adjacent IgE molecules on mast cells.
2. **Degranulation** — Within **seconds to minutes**, mast cells release pre-formed mediators:
   - **Histamine** (vasodilation, bronchoconstriction)
   - **Tryptase** (tissue remodeling marker)
   - **Heparin** (anticoagulant effect)
3. **Late-phase response** — Newly synthesized mediators (**leukotrienes C4, D4, E4**, **prostaglandin D2**, cytokines IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) are released over **4–8 hours**, sustaining inflammation.

## 2.2 Biochemical Pathway of Histamine Action

Histamine exerts effects through four receptor subtypes, of which **H1** is most relevant in food allergy:

$$ \text{Histamine} + \text{H1 Receptor} \rightarrow \text{G}_q \text{ protein activation} $$

$$ \downarrow $$

$$ \text{PLC}\beta \rightarrow \text{IP}_3 + \text{DAG} $$

$$ \downarrow $$

$$ \text{Ca}^{2+} \uparrow \rightarrow \text{Smooth muscle contraction, vascular permeability} \uparrow $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Connection:** Antihistamines like **cetirizine** are H1-receptor **inverse agonists** that stabilize the receptor in its inactive state, preventing histamine binding — hence their therapeutic utility.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitization threshold | $D_{sens} = \frac{C \cdot V}{M_b}$ | mg allergen / kg body weight |
| Eliciting dose (EDp) | Probability of reaction in p% population | mg protein |
| IgE binding affinity | $K_a = \frac{[IgE \cdot Fc\varepsilon RI]}{[IgE][Fc\varepsilon RI]}$ | $\text{M}^{-1}$ |
| Histamine release kinetics | $\frac{dH}{dt} = k_{deg} \cdot [Mast]_{activated}$ | ng/mL per minute |
| Anaphylaxis severity score | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i$ | Weighted sum of symptoms |
| NOAEL for allergen (model) | $NOAEL = \frac{LOAEL}{UF}$ | Uncertainty Factor (UF) typically = 10 |
| Risk characterization | $R = \frac{Exposure}{RfD}$ | Dimensionless ratio |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Industrial Utility

This immunological framework underpins critical food industry operations:
- **Allergen Management Plans (AMPs)** in HACCP-based food processing units.
- **Threshold-setting** by the **VITAL (Voluntary Incidental Trace Allergen Labeling) Scientific Expert Panel** which informs food labeling regulations.
- **Detection assay design** — ELISA kits for quantifying peanut protein at ppm (parts per million) levels rely on IgE-antibody mimetics.
- **Cross-contact prevention** engineering in chocolate, ice-cream, and bakery production lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Population Eliciting Dose (EDp)

The **Population Eliciting Dose** is the dose of an allergen predicted to elicit an allergic reaction in a specified percentage (p) of the allergic population. It is derived from log-normal dose-response modeling.

### Step 1: Define the cumulative dose-response function

$$ F(d) = \Phi\left( \frac{\ln(d) - \mu}{\sigma} \right) $$

where:
- $\Phi$ is the standard normal cumulative distribution function
- $d$ is the allergen dose (mg protein)
- $\mu$ is the mean of $\ln(d)$ in the allergic population
- $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of $\ln(d)$

### Step 2: Invert for EDp

Setting $F(d_p) = p / 100$, we solve for $d_p$:

$$ \frac{p}{100} = \Phi\left( \frac{\ln(d_p) - \mu}{\sigma} \right) $$

$$ \frac{\ln(d_p) - \mu}{\sigma} = \Phi^{-1}\left(\frac{p}{100}\right) $$

$$ \ln(d_p) = \mu + \sigma \cdot \Phi^{-1}\left(\frac{p}{100}\right) $$

$$ d_p = \exp\left[ \mu + \sigma \cdot \Phi^{-1}\left(\frac{p}{100}\right) \right] $$

### Step 3: Numerical Example — Peanut Allergen

For peanut protein, population studies yield $\mu = 1.45$ and $\sigma = 1.90$ (log mg protein). Calculate $ED_{10}$ (dose predicted to affect 10% of allergic population):

$$ \Phi^{-1}(0.10) = -1.2816 $$

$$ \ln(d_{10}) = 1.45 + (1.90)(-1.2816) $$

$$ \ln(d_{10}) = 1.45 - 2.4350 = -0.9850 $$

$$ d_{10} = \exp(-0.9850) \approx 0.373 \text{ mg peanut protein} $$

### Step 4: Interpretation

This means that approximately **10% of peanut-allergic individuals would react to a dose as low as 0.373 mg of peanut protein** — roughly **one-hundredth of a single peanut kernel**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Sensitization Probability Using Bayesian Updating

A probabilistic model for sensitization onset uses prior population susceptibility $P(S)$ and update upon exposure event $E$:

$$ P(S \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert S) \cdot P(S)}{P(E)} $$

By the law of total probability:

$$ P(E) = P(E \vert S) \cdot P(S) + P(E \vert \neg S) \cdot P(\neg S) $$

Substituting back:

$$ P(S \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert S) \cdot P(S)}{P(E \vert S) \cdot P(S) + P(E \vert \neg S) \cdot [1 - P(S)]} $$

For a high-risk allergen like peanut: $P(S) = 0.02$, $P(E \mid S) = 0.85$, $P(E \mid \neg S) = 0.01$:

$$ P(E) = (0.85)(0.02) + (0.01)(0.98) = 0.0170 + 0.0098 = 0.0268 $$

$$ P(S \vert E) = \frac{(0.85)(0.02)}{0.0268} = \frac{0.0170}{0.0268} \approx 0.634 $$

This implies that a positive exposure event raises the posterior probability of sensitization from a 2% baseline to **63.4%** — a **31.7-fold increase** — illustrating why early-life allergen exposure is epidemiologically critical.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Hypersensitivity Types

| Feature | Type I | Type II | Type III | Type IV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mediator | IgE | IgG, IgM | IgG complexes | T-cells, Macrophages |
| Onset | < 30 min | Hours | Hours–Days | 48–72 hours |
| Cells involved | Mast, Basophil | Macrophage, NK | Neutrophils | CD4⁺, CD8⁺ T-cells |
| Example | Peanut anaphylaxis | Drug-induced cytopenia | Serum sickness | Contact dermatitis |
| Relevance to food | **Highest** | Low | Low | Moderate (celiac) |
| Antibody/complement role | IgE only | IgG + Complement | IgG + Complement | None (cell-mediated) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Celiac Disease Special Note:** Although gluten-induced celiac disease involves both **IgA antibodies** (against tissue transglutaminase) and **CD4⁺ T-cell** (Type IV) responses, the KTU syllabus classifies it primarily as a **Type IV hypersensitivity** with secondary humoral components.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Coombs and Gell Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["FOOD HYPERSENSITIVITY"]
    rootB["Non-Immunological INTOLERANCE"]
    rootA --> nodeB["TYPE I IgE Mediated Immediate"]
    rootA --> nodeC["TYPE II Cytotoxic Antibody"]
    rootA --> nodeD["TYPE III Immune Complex"]
    rootA --> nodeE["TYPE IV Cell Mediated Delayed"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Peanut Anaphylaxis"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Shellfish Urticaria"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Milk IgE Allergy"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Rare in Food"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Rare in Food"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["Celiac Disease"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Contact Dermatitis"]
    rootB --> nodeF1["Lactose Intolerance"]
    rootB --> nodeF2["Tyramine Reaction"]
```

## 4.2 IgE-Mediated Allergic Reaction Pathway (Sensitization → Elicitation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1["Allergen Ingestion"]
    step2["APC Processing and MHC II Presentation"]
    step3["Th2 Cell Differentiation"]
    step4["B Cell IgE Class Switching"]
    step5["IgE Binding to Mast Cell Fc Epsilon RI"]
    step6["Mast Cell Sensitized Stage Complete"]
    step7["Re Exposure to Same Allergen"]
    step8["IgE Crosslinking on Mast Cell"]
    step9["Calcium Influx and Degranulation"]
    step10["Histamine and Tryptase Release"]
    step11["Late Phase Leukotrienes and Cytokines"]
    step12["Clinical Symptoms Urticaria Anaphylaxis"]
    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7 --> step8 --> step9 --> step10 --> step11 --> step12
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Food Allergy Risk Assessment

```mermaid
graph TD
    blockA["INPUT: Food Composition Data"]
    blockB["Hazard Identification: Allergen Detection"]
    blockC["Dose Response Assessment: EDp Modeling"]
    blockD["Exposure Assessment: Dietary Intake"]
    blockE["Risk Characterization: Margin of Exposure"]
    blockF["OUTPUT: Labeling and Regulatory Action"]
    blockA --> blockB --> blockC --> blockD --> blockE --> blockF
    blockC --> blockG["Population Susceptibility Data"]
    blockD --> blockH["Consumption Patterns"]
    blockG --> blockC
    blockH --> blockD
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define food allergy. Differentiate it from food intolerance with two key distinguishing points.**

**Model Answer:**
Food allergy is an adverse immune-mediated reaction to a food protein that occurs reproducibly upon exposure in a sensitized individual.

**Distinguishing points:**
1. **Mechanism** — Food allergy involves the immune system (IgE, T-cells), whereas food intolerance is non-immunological (e.g., enzyme deficiency).
2. **Dose dependency** — Allergic reactions can be triggered by trace quantities of the allergen (mg or µg levels), whereas intolerances generally require larger quantities of the offending substance.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**List any six common food allergens responsible for IgE-mediated food allergies.**

**Model Answer:**
The "Big Eight" food allergens identified by international regulatory bodies:
1. Milk
2. Eggs
3. Fish
4. Shellfish (crustaceans and mollusks)
5. Tree nuts (almonds, cashews, walnuts)
6. Peanuts
7. Wheat
8. Soybeans

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Classify hypersensitivity reactions according to Coombs and Gell. Explain in detail the mechanism of Type I IgE-mediated hypersensitivity with a suitable food allergy example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the clinical manifestations, diagnosis, and management strategies of food allergy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer (a):
The Coombs and Gell classification divides hypersensitivity into four types. The focus here is Type I:

**Mechanism of Type I Hypersensitivity:**

**Sensitization Phase:**
- Allergen exposure → APC processing → MHC II presentation
- Th2 cell activation with IL-4 and IL-13 secretion
- B-cell class switching from IgM to allergen-specific IgE
- IgE binds to high-affinity FcεRI receptors on mast cells and basophils

**Elicitation Phase:**
- Re-exposure causes IgE crosslinking on mast cell surface
- Calcium influx triggers degranulation
- Release of histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, prostaglandin D2
- Immediate clinical symptoms appear within minutes

**Example:** Peanut anaphylaxis — even trace exposure (≥ 0.2 mg protein) in sensitized individuals can cause urticaria, bronchospasm, hypotension, and potentially fatal anaphylactic shock.

#### Model Answer (b):
**Clinical Manifestations:**
- **Cutaneous:** Urticaria, angioedema, eczema
- **Gastrointestinal:** Vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain
- **Respiratory:** Rhinitis, bronchospasm, laryngeal edema
- **Systemic:** Anaphylaxis (life-threatening)

**Diagnosis:**
- Clinical history and skin-prick test (SPT)
- Serum-specific IgE assay (ImmunoCAP)
- Oral food challenge (gold standard, conducted under medical supervision)
- Component-resolved diagnostics for molecular allergen identification

**Management:**
- Strict avoidance of the offending allergen
- Pharmacotherapy: Antihistamines (H1 blockers), corticosteroids
- **Emergency:** Intramuscular epinephrine (0.3–0.5 mg adult dose) for anaphylaxis
- Allergen-specific immunotherapy (emerging modality)
- Patient education on allergen labeling and emergency action plans

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain the concept of food allergy risk assessment. Derive the population eliciting dose equation and calculate ED₁₀ for a hypothetical allergen with population parameters μ = 1.20 and σ = 1.85. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the major food allergens, their molecular characteristics, and the principles of allergen labeling as per FSSAI / Codex Alimentarius. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer (a):
**Concept of Risk Assessment:**
Food allergy risk assessment is a structured process for evaluating the probability and severity of allergic reactions in a population. It has four components:
1. Hazard identification
2. Dose-response assessment
3. Exposure assessment
4. Risk characterization

**Derivation of EDp:**
The dose-response for allergic reactions follows a log-normal distribution. The cumulative probability $F(d)$ of reaction at dose $d$ is:

$$ F(d) = \Phi\left( \frac{\ln(d) - \mu}{\sigma} \right) $$

Setting $F(d_p) = p/100$ and inverting:

$$ d_p = \exp\left[ \mu + \sigma \cdot \Phi^{-1}\left(\frac{p}{100}\right) \right] $$

**Numerical Calculation:**
Given $\mu = 1.20$, $\sigma = 1.85$, calculate $ED_{10}$:

$$ \Phi^{-1}(0.10) = -1.2816 $$

$$ \ln(d_{10}) = 1.20 + (1.85)(-1.2816) = 1.20 - 2.3710 = -1.1710 $$

$$ d_{10} = \exp(-1.1710) \approx 0.310 \text{ mg protein} $$

**Interpretation:** Approximately 10% of the allergic population would react to as little as 0.31 mg of this allergen, demonstrating that regulatory thresholds must be set at extremely low levels.

#### Model Answer (b):
**Major Food Allergens — Molecular Characteristics:**

| Allergen | Molecular Weight | Stability | Major Allergen |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peanut | 10–70 kDa | Heat/stable | Ara h 1, Ara h 2 |
| Cow's milk | 14–80 kDa | Heat-sensitive | β-lactoglobulin, casein |
| Egg | 28–45 kDa | Partially heat-sensitive | Ovomucoid, ovalbumin |
| Soy | 7–71 kDa | Variable | Gly m 5, Gly m 6 |
| Wheat | 12–65 kDa | Heat-stable | ω-5 gliadin |
| Shrimp | 21–36 kDa | Heat-stable (tropomyosin) | Pen a 1 |

**Allergen Labeling Principles:**
- **FSSAI (India)** mandates declaration of eight major allergens under the Food Safety and Standards (Labelling and Display) Regulations, 2020.
- **Codex Alimentarius** (CAC/GL 26-1999) provides a global reference framework.
- **Precautionary Allergen Labeling (PAL)** uses phrases like "May contain traces of…" — though VITAL-based POP (Pictograms) systems are increasingly replacing ambiguous text.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing intolerance with allergy** — Marks are deducted (up to 2 marks) if students do not clearly emphasize the **immune-mediated nature** of food allergy.
> 2. **Skipping the sensitization phase** — In any mechanism-based question on Type I hypersensitivity, examiners require **both** sensitization AND elicitation phases described separately. Omission of sensitization alone causes a 3-mark deduction.
> 3. **Incorrect units** — Always specify dose in **mg of protein**, not mg of food matrix. This unit-precision is explicitly checked.
> 4. **Forgetting emergency management** — In management questions, the explicit mention of **intramuscular epinephrine as first-line for anaphylaxis** is mandatory. Missing it loses 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Food allergy = immune-mediated**; **Food intolerance = non-immunological** (most tested distinction).
- **Coombs and Gell Type I** (IgE-mediated) is the most relevant hypersensitivity in food toxicology — always describe sensitization AND elicitation phases.
- **Mast cells and basophils** are the effector cells; **histamine** is the principal pre-formed mediator.
- **H1 receptor activation** triggers smooth muscle contraction, vasodilation, and increased vascular permeability.
- **The "Big Eight" allergens:** Milk, Egg, Fish, Shellfish, Tree nuts, Peanuts, Wheat, Soy.
- **Anaphylaxis** is the most severe manifestation — treated with **IM epinephrine** (0.3–0.5 mg adults).
- **Population Eliciting Dose (EDp)** equation:
$$ d_p = \exp\left[ \mu + \sigma \cdot \Phi^{-1}\left(\frac{p}{100}\right) \right] $$
- **VITAL 3.0 reference doses** for major allergens are typically in the **0.03 – 1.0 mg protein** range.
- **Diagnosis triad:** Clinical history + Skin-prick test + Serum-specific IgE; **Oral food challenge** is the gold standard.
- **Risk assessment components:** Hazard ID → Dose-response → Exposure assessment → Risk characterization.
- **Labeling regulations:** FSSAI 2020 (India), Codex CAC/GL 26-1999 (international), VITAL 3.0 (threshold-based).
- **Celiac disease** is a Type IV (cell-mediated) hypersensitivity to gluten, often co-existing with IgA anti-tTG antibodies.
- **Cross-reactivity** is common among related allergens (e.g., latex-fruit syndrome, peanut-tree nut cross-reactivity).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanisms of Allergic-Type Reactions and Allergenic Components in Foods, Food Disorders Associated with Metabolism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT634 - FOOD TOXICOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanisms of Allergic-Type Reactions and Allergenic Components in Foods, Food Disorders Associated with Metabolism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Food Hypersensitivity and Allergy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (FAO/WHO Codex Alimentarius, 1999):**
> **Food Allergy** is an adverse health effect arising from a specific immune response that occurs reproducibly on exposure to a given food. It is a subset of **Food Hypersensitivity**, which broadly denotes any reproducible adverse reaction to a food at a dose tolerated by normal individuals.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine your body's immune system is a highly trained **security guard** posted at the gates of a fortress (your body). Normally, the guard checks IDs of incoming "visitors" (food proteins) and lets harmless ones — like an apple slice — pass without trouble. 

In a **food-allergic individual**, however, the security guard has a *paranoid glitch*. It mistakes a harmless food protein (an *allergen*) for a dangerous intruder. It sounds a deafening alarm, mobilises heavy weapons (histamine, leukotrienes, antibodies), and ends up damaging the fortress's own walls (the body’s tissues) in the panic. This friendly-fire is what we call an **allergic reaction**.

### Classification of Adverse Food Reactions (AAAAI / KTU Syllabus)

| Broad Category | Mechanism | Example | Immune? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Food Allergy** (Hypersensitivity) | Immune-mediated | Peanut anaphylaxis, Celiac disease | ✅ Yes |
| **Food Intolerance** | Non-immune (enzyme/metabolic) | Lactose intolerance, Favism | ❌ No |
| **Food Aversion / Pharmacological** | Direct chemical action | Caffeine jitter, Tyramine migraine | ❌ No |
| **Food Toxicity** | Toxin in food | Mushroom poisoning | ❌ No |

---

## 1.2 The Coombs & Gell Classification of Hypersensitivity

> [!NOTE]
> **Coombs & Gell (1963)** classified hypersensitivity reactions into **four types** based on the immune mechanism and the time course of the reaction. Food toxicology primarily concerns **Type I** (IgE-mediated, immediate) and **Type IV** (cell-mediated, delayed — e.g., Celiac), with secondary involvement of Type III.

### Conceptual Analogy for the Four Types
- **Type I** — *Burglar alarm ringing the moment a door opens* (immediate, IgE + mast cells).
- **Type II** — *Body's own cells wrongly tagged for destruction by antibodies* (cytotoxic).
- **Type III** — *Antibody–antigen debris clogging up the drainage system* (immune complex deposition).
- **Type IV** — *SWAT team arriving 24–72 hours late* (T-cell mediated, delayed).

---

## 1.3 Defining Allergenic Food Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Allergen (Codex definition):** A normally harmless food protein (or glycoprotein) capable of eliciting, in susceptible individuals, a specific immunological reaction leading to characteristic symptoms.

### The "Big-8 / Big-9" Allergen List (FDA, 2004 / FALCPA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The U.S. **Food Allergen Labeling and Consumer Protection Act (FALCPA)** identifies **8 major food allergens**, later expanded to **9** by the FAO/WHO Codex and EU regulations. These account for **> 90% of all documented food allergies worldwide**.

| # | Allergen | Common Allergenic Proteins | Typical Threshold (mg protein) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cow's milk** | Caseins (αs1-, αs2-, β-, κ-), β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin | **0.1 – 1.0 mg** |
| 2 | **Egg** | Ovomucoid (Gal d 1), Ovalbumin (Gal d 2), Ovotransferrin, Lysozyme | **0.1 mg** |
| 3 | **Fish** | Parvalbumin (Gad c 1) – Ca²⁺-binding, heat-stable | **0.01 – 1.0 mg** |
| 4 | **Shellfish (Crustaceans)** | Tropomyosin (Pen a 1) – pan-allergen | **0.1 – 1.0 mg** |
| 5 | **Tree nuts** | 2S albumins, 7S & 11S globulins, LTP | **0.01 – 1.0 mg** |
| 6 | **Peanuts** | Ara h 1, Ara h 2 (2S albumin), Ara h 3, Ara h 6, Ara h 8 (PR-10) | **0.1 – 1.0 mg** |
| 7 | **Wheat** | Gliadins, Glutenins, ω-5 gliadin (Tri a 19), LTP, α-amylase inhibitor | **0.1 mg** |
| 8 | **Soybeans** | Gly m 5, Gly m 6, Gly m 8 (2S albumin), profilin | **0.1 – 1.0 mg** |
| 9 | **Sesame** (added 2023 US) | 2S albumins, oleosins, profilins | **0.1 mg** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitisation threshold** of **0.1 mg of total protein** is a benchmark used in KTU/Codex VITAL (Voluntary Incidental Trace Allergen Labelling) 2.0 risk assessment for population-level allergen management.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Mechanistic Pathways of Allergic-Type Reactions

### 2.1.1 TYPE I — IgE-Mediated Immediate Hypersensitivity (The Classic Food Allergy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the mechanism behind *anaphylaxis*, *oral allergy syndrome (OAS)*, and most acute urticarial food reactions. Onset: **seconds to 2 hours** post-ingestion.

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Sensitisation Phase** (first exposure, no symptoms):
   - Allergen is taken up by Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs) — dendritic cells of gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT).
   - APC presents peptide to **naïve CD4⁺ T-helper cell (Th0)**.
   - In presence of **IL-4**, Th0 differentiates into **Th2 cell**.
   - Th2 releases cytokines: **IL-4, IL-5, IL-13**.
   - **IL-4** drives B-cell class-switching to produce **allergen-specific IgE antibodies**.

2. **Effector Phase** (subsequent exposure):
   - Allergen-specific IgE binds to **high-affinity FcεRI receptors** on **mast cells** (in tissue) and **basophils** (in blood).
   - Cross-linking of ≥ 2 adjacent IgE molecules by multivalent allergen triggers **degranulation**.
   - Pre-formed mediators released: **histamine, tryptase, heparin, chymase**.
   - Newly synthesised mediators: **leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4), prostaglandins (PGD2), platelet-activating factor (PAF), cytokines (TNF-α)**.

3. **Clinical Manifestation** — the **"ITCH-SWELL-WHEEZE-DROP"** tetrad:
   - Skin: Urticaria (hives), angioedema, pruritus, flushing
   - GI: Vomiting, abdominal cramps, diarrhoea
   - Respiratory: Rhinorrhoea, bronchospasm, laryngeal oedema
   - Cardiovascular: **Anaphylactic shock** — hypotension, tachycardia, collapse

**Two-Phase Anaphylaxis:** Biphasic reactions occur in **~5–20%** of cases; symptoms recur 1–72 hours after the initial episode due to a "second wave" of mast-cell mediator release.

---

### 2.1.2 TYPE II — Cytotoxic Hypersensitivity

> [!NOTE]
> **Rare in classical food allergy**, but relevant in *Haemolytic anaemia* induced by certain food contaminants (e.g., mould toxins), and in *Henoch–Schönlein purpura* (IgA-mediated). Antibodies (IgG/IgM) bind antigens on cell surfaces → complement activation → cell lysis (CDC) or antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC).

**Food-Associated Examples:**
- Favism (G6PD-deficiency) — sometimes classified here due to oxidative damage of RBC membranes
- Heiner syndrome (pulmonary haemosiderosis) — cow's milk antibodies reacting with alveolar endothelium
- Drug–hapten food interactions (e.g., penicillin residues in milk → RBC haemolysis)

---

### 2.1.3 TYPE III — Immune Complex Hypersensitivity

> [!NOTE]
> **Antigen–antibody (immune) complexes** deposit in tissues (vessel walls, glomeruli) and activate complement (C3a, C5a), recruiting neutrophils → tissue damage.

**Food-Associated Examples:**
- **Coeliac disease** (also Type IV) — gluten–anti-tTG immune complex deposition
- **Arthus-like reactions** to injected food proteins (rare)
- Serum sickness from food-borne drug residues (sulfonamides)

---

### 2.1.4 TYPE IV — Cell-Mediated (Delayed) Hypersensitivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coeliac disease (gluten-sensitive enteropathy)** is the principal food-related Type IV disorder. Onset: **24–72 hours** post-exposure. Mediated by **T-lymphocytes** (CD4⁺ Th1 and CD8⁺ cytotoxic T-cells) rather than antibodies.

**Mechanistic Steps:**
1. **Sensitisation** — Deamidated gliadin peptides (via tissue transglutaminase, **tTG2**) bind to **HLA-DQ2/DQ8** MHC-II molecules on APCs.
2. **CD4⁺ Th1 activation** → release of **IFN-γ, TNF-α** → chronic inflammation, villous atrophy.
3. **CD8⁺ intraepithelial lymphocytes (IELs)** directly kill enterocytes (NK-receptor mediated).
4. **Crypt hyperplasia + villous atrophy + intraepithelial lymphocytosis** = the Marsh grading pathology.

> **Diagnostic Marker:** Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA (anti-tTG IgA) antibodies, with **> 95% sensitivity and specificity**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Reference Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All values below are **regulatory thresholds / reference values** used in KTU board exam numerical questions on threshold dose calculations.

| Symbol | Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{ED}_{50}$ | Eliciting Dose for 50% of allergic population | Dose at which 50% of sensitised individuals react | mg total protein |
| $\text{NOAEL}_{\text{allergen}}$ | No Observed Adverse Effect Level | Highest dose with no reaction in test group | mg / kg body weight |
| $\text{LOAEL}_{\text{allergen}}$ | Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level | Lowest dose producing a reaction | mg / kg body weight |
| $V_{\text{VITAL}}$ | VITAL 2.0 reference dose | Population-based threshold | **0.1 – 1.0 mg** |
| $\text{Sf}_{\text{individual}}$ | Safety factor for individual variability | $\text{LOAEL} / \text{Sf}$ | 10 – 100 (dimensionless) |
| $\text{AF}_{\text{allergen}}$ | Allergenicity factor (digestibility, stability) | Qualitative | Low / Med / High |
| $\text{MAR}_{\text{serum}}$ | Minimum eliciting reactivity | Skin-prick wheal diameter ≥ 3 mm | mm |
| $\text{IgE}_{\text{serum}}$ | Specific IgE titre (ImmunoCAP) | Class 0 – 6 | kUₐ / L |
| $\text{MTT}_{\text{histamine}}$ | Mast cell tryptase (anaphylaxis marker) | Peaks 1–2 h post-event | µg / L |
| $\text{Hp}_{\text{breath}}$ | Hydrogen breath test (lactose) | Rise ≥ 20 ppm over baseline | ppm |

### Reference Equations for Threshold Dose Calculation

**Population Threshold Dose (VITAL-style approach):**

$$\text{ED}_{p} = \text{LOAEL}_{\text{study}} \times \text{Sf}_{\text{population}}$$

**Individual Risk Dose:**

$$\text{Dose}_{\text{risk}} = \frac{\text{ED}_{p}}{\text{Sf}_{\text{individual}} \times \text{Sf}_{\text{cooking}} \times \text{Sf}_{\text{matrix}}}$$

> Where:
> - $\text{Sf}_{\text{cooking}}$ = thermal processing effect factor (typically 0.5 – 2.0; *peanuts* → 0.5; *milk* → 10 reduction)
> - $\text{Sf}_{\text{matrix}}$ = food matrix effect (fat-bound allergens → × 1.5; water-soluble → × 1.0)

**Histamine Equivalent Threshold for Scombroid Poisoning:**

$$\text{HIT}_{\text{toxic}} \geq 50 \text{ mg per 100 g of fish muscle}$$

### Allergen Structural & Physicochemical Property Matrix

| Property | Clinical / Industrial Consequence |
|---|---|
| **Molecular weight 10–70 kDa** | Optimal for B-cell receptor cross-linking |
| **Glycoprotein (carbohydrate moiety)** | Enhanced uptake by APCs via lectin receptors |
| **Heat-stable (e.g., Ara h 2, ovomucoid, parvalbumin)** | Persistent after cooking → true allergy, not OAS |
| **Heat-labile (e.g., Bet v 1 homologues)** | Destroyed by cooking → typically mild oral allergy syndrome |
| **Acid-stable / pepsin-resistant** | Survives gastric digestion → reaches gut immune system intact |
| **Water-soluble** | Easily leached into cooking water → cross-contamination risk |
| **Lipid-binding (2S albumins, LTPs)** | May survive frying in oil → fried-food reactions |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

- **Food Manufacturing**: Allergen management plans (HACCP-based) — segregation, cleaning validation, ELISA-based detection limits (typically **< 2.5 ppm** for milk, **< 5 ppm** for gluten).
- **Diagnostic Technology**: Component-Resolved Diagnostics (CRD) using recombinant allergens (e.g., ImmunoCAP ISAC) — distinguishes true sensitisation from cross-reactivity.
- **Immunotherapy**: Oral Immunotherapy (OIT) — graded dosing from **0.1 mg → 300 mg** over 6–12 months to desensitise.
- **Food Labelling**: Allergen labelling under FALCPA / EU FIC 1169/2011 / Indian FSSAI (2020) — "Contains" statements mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical: Population Threshold Dose (VITAL 2.0) for Peanut

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Board Question Type:** "Calculate the population reference dose and the individual safe dose for peanut allergen given the following data..."

### Given Data
- LOAEL from clinical study: **$\text{LOAEL} = 2.0$ mg of peanut protein** (lowest dose at which allergic individuals reacted in DBPCFC)
- Standard population safety factor: $\text{Sf}_{p} = 10$ (extrapolation from LOAEL to NOAEL)
- Individual variability safety factor: $\text{Sf}_{i} = 10$
- Cooking factor (roasted peanut): $\text{Sf}_{c} = 0.7$ (roasting *enhances* Ara h 2 allergenicity)
- Matrix factor (chocolate bar with high fat): $\text{Sf}_{m} = 1.5$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert LOAEL to population-level ED:**
$$\text{ED}_{p} = \text{LOAEL} \times \frac{1}{\text{Sf}_{p}}$$
$$\text{ED}_{p} = 2.0 \text{ mg} \times \frac{1}{10}$$
$$\boxed{\text{ED}_{p} = 0.20 \text{ mg peanut protein}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply individual safety factor to obtain individual ED:**
$$\text{ED}_{i} = \text{ED}_{p} \times \frac{1}{\text{Sf}_{i}}$$
$$\text{ED}_{i} = 0.20 \text{ mg} \times \frac{1}{10}$$
$$\text{ED}_{i} = 0.020 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 3 — Apply food-processing and matrix correction factors:**
$$\text{Dose}_{\text{final}} = \text{ED}_{i} \times \text{Sf}_{c} \times \text{Sf}_{m}$$
$$\text{Dose}_{\text{final}} = 0.020 \text{ mg} \times 0.7 \times 1.5$$
$$\text{Dose}_{\text{final}} = 0.020 \times 1.05$$
$$\boxed{\text{Dose}_{\text{final}} = 0.021 \text{ mg peanut protein}}$$

**Step 4 — Express per kg body weight (assume 60 kg adult):**
$$\text{Dose}_{\text{BW}} = \frac{0.021 \text{ mg}}{60 \text{ kg}} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mg/kg BW}$$

**Step 5 — Practical interpretation:**
This 0.021 mg of protein translates to **~ 0.05 mg of whole peanut** (peanut is ~ 25% protein) — i.e., less than **one-tenth of a single peanut kernel** can trigger a reaction in the most sensitive 1% of the allergic population.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic note:** In VITAL 2.0, peanut reference dose = **0.2 mg protein** (ED₀₁ for 1% of population). The above derivation yields a *conservative* (more protective) individual dose by cascading three safety factors.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Hydrogen Breath Test (Lactose Intolerance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Board Question:** "A 70-kg adult consumes 50 g lactose (standard test dose). Breath H₂ rises from 8 ppm baseline to 38 ppm at 90 min. Calculate the rise and interpret the result."

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the rise in ppm:**
$$\Delta \text{H}_{2} = \text{H}_{2(\text{peak})} - \text{H}_{2(\text{baseline})}$$
$$\Delta \text{H}_{2} = 38 \text{ ppm} - 8 \text{ ppm}$$
$$\boxed{\Delta \text{H}_{2} = 30 \text{ ppm}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply diagnostic threshold:**
The cut-off for **positive lactose malabsorption** is:
$$\Delta \text{H}_{2} \geq 20 \text{ ppm over baseline}$$

**Step 3 — Compare and interpret:**
$$30 \text{ ppm} \geq 20 \text{ ppm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Test is POSITIVE}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate per-kg dose for personalisation:**
$$\text{Dose}_{\text{BW}} = \frac{50 \text{ g}}{70 \text{ kg}} = 0.714 \text{ g/kg BW}$$

> This dose (0.7 g/kg) is well above the *symptom threshold* of **~ 0.2 g/kg BW** for adult lactase non-persistence populations (60–70% global prevalence).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Metabolic Food Disorders vs. Immune Allergies

| Feature | Lactose Intolerance | Coeliac Disease | PKU | Favism (G6PD⁻) | Galactosaemia |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Metabolic (non-immune) | Type IV hypersensitivity | Metabolic enzyme deficiency | Enzyme deficiency (RBC) | Metabolic enzyme |
| **Trigger molecule** | Lactose (disaccharide) | Gliadin (prolamin) | Phenylalanine | Vicine / Convicine (Fava beans) | Galactose-1-phosphate |
| **Defective enzyme / system** | **Lactase (LCT gene)** | tTG2 + HLA-DQ2/8 | **Phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH)** | **Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase** | **GALT (Galactose-1-P uridyltransferase)** |
| **Inheritance** | Autosomal recessive (adult-onset) | Polygenic with HLA | Autosomal recessive | X-linked recessive | Autosomal recessive |
| **Onset** | Hours | Days – weeks | Years (untreated) | Hours post-fava | Days (infancy) |
| **Diagnostic marker** | H₂ breath test (+ve if ΔH₂ ≥ 20 ppm) | Anti-tTG IgA + duodenal biopsy | Plasma Phe > 1200 µmol/L | G6PD assay; Heinz bodies | GALT activity; urinary galactitol |
| **Management** | Lactose-restricted diet, lactase supplements | **Strict, lifelong gluten-free diet** | Phe-restricted diet; aspartame avoidance | Avoid fava beans, oxidative drugs | Galactose-free formula (soy-based) |
| **Global prevalence** | **65 – 70%** of adults | **~ 1%** | **1 : 10,000 – 15,000** | **~ 400 million** carriers | **1 : 30,000 – 60,000** |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Histamine Calculation in Scombroid Poisoning

**Scombroid fish** (tuna, mackerel, mahi-mahi) harbour *Morganella morganii* and other histamine-producing bacteria. They convert **histidine → histamine** via histidine decarboxylase.

$$\text{Histidine} \xrightarrow{\text{histidine decarboxylase}} \text{Histamine} + \text{CO}_2$$

**Toxic threshold (FDA):**
$$\text{Histamine} \geq 50 \text{ mg}/100\text{g}$$

**Sample calculation:** Tuna sample mass = 250 g; histamine measured = 145 mg.

$$\text{Concentration} = \frac{145 \text{ mg}}{250 \text{ g}} \times 100 = 58 \text{ mg}/100\text{g}$$

$$58 \text{ mg}/100\text{g} \geq 50 \text{ mg}/100\text{g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TOXIC / UNSAFE}$$

> This produces a *pseudo-allergic* reaction (histamine intoxication, not IgE-mediated), with flushing, headache, diarrhoea, urticaria — indistinguishable from true fish allergy *clinically*, but **without immunological memory**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Type I (IgE-Mediated) Hypersensitivity Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[First Exposure: Allergen Ingestion] --> B[Antigen Presenting Cell APC Uptake]
    B --> C[APC presents peptide to Th0 cell]
    C --> D{IL-4 present in microenvironment?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Th0 differentiates into Th2 cell]
    D -- No --> F[Tolerance or Treg induction]
    E --> G[Th2 releases IL-4, IL-5, IL-13]
    G --> H[B-cell class switching to IgE]
    H --> I[Allergen specific IgE released]
    I --> J[IgE binds Fc epsilon RI on Mast Cell]
    J --> K[Sensitised Individual: No Symptoms Yet]

    K --> L[Second Exposure: Same Allergen]
    L --> M[Allergen cross links two adjacent IgE on mast cell]
    M --> N[Mast Cell Degranulation]
    N --> O[Pre formed mediators: Histamine Tryptase Heparin]
    N --> P[Newly synthesised: Leukotrienes PGD2 PAF TNF alpha]
    O --> Q[Clinical Symptoms]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Skin: Urticaria Angioedema]
    Q --> S[GI: Vomiting Cramps Diarrhoea]
    Q --> T[Respiratory: Wheeze Laryngeal Oedema]
    Q --> U[Cardiovascular: Anaphylactic Shock]
```

## 4.2 Coombs and Gell Classification Overview

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hypersensitivity Reactions] --> B[Type I Immediate IgE]
    A --> C[Type II Cytotoxic IgG IgM]
    A --> D[Type III Immune Complex]
    A --> E[Type IV Delayed T cell]

    B --> B1[Onset: Seconds to 2 hr]
    B --> B2[Example: Peanut Anaphylaxis]
    B --> B3[Mediators: Histamine Leukotrienes]

    C --> C1[Onset: Minutes to Hours]
    C --> C2[Example: Heiner Syndrome]
    C --> C3[Mechanism: Complement Lysis]

    D --> D1[Onset: 4 to 8 hours]
    D --> D2[Example: Serum Sickness]
    D --> D3[Mechanism: Immune Complex Deposition]

    E --> E1[Onset: 24 to 72 hours]
    E --> E2[Example: Coeliac Disease]
    E --> E3[Mechanism: Th1 CD8 T cell]
```

## 4.3 Coeliac Disease Pathogenesis Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gluten Ingestion Wheat Barley Rye] --> B[Gluten proteins reach small intestine]
    B --> C[Gliadin peptides resist digestion]
    C --> D[Tissue Transglutaminase tTG2 deamidates gliadin]
    D --> E[Deamidated gliadin binds HLA DQ2 or DQ8 on APC]
    E --> F[CD4 Th1 cell activation]
    F --> G[Release of IFN gamma and TNF alpha]
    G --> H[Chronic inflammation of lamina propria]
    F --> I[CD8 Intraepithelial Lymphocyte activation]
    I --> J[Direct killing of enterocytes]
    H --> K[Villous atrophy]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Malabsorption: Iron Folate Calcium Fat]
    L --> M[Clinical: Diarrhoea Steatorrhoea Weight Loss Anaemia]
```

## 4.4 Food Hypersensitivity Decision Tree for Diagnosis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient presents with adverse food reaction] --> B{Is it immune mediated?}
    B -- No --> C[Food Intolerance or Toxicity]
    C --> C1[Enzyme test e.g. H2 breath]
    C --> C2[Metabolic screening e.g. PKU G6PD]
    B -- Yes --> D{IgE or Non IgE mechanism?}
    D -- IgE Type I --> E[Type I Immediate Allergy]
    E --> E1[Skin Prick Test]
    E --> E2[Serum Specific IgE ImmunoCAP]
    E --> E3[Oral Food Challenge Gold Standard]
    D -- Non IgE --> F[Type III or IV]
    F --> F1[Anti tTG IgA for Coeliac]
    F --> F2[Patch Test for Type IV]
    F --> F3[Duodenal Biopsy Marsh grading]
```

## 4.5 Allergen Labelling and Detection Limit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Intake] --> B[Allergen Segregation]
    B --> C[Dedicated Production Line]
    C --> D[Cleaning Validation ELISA]
    D --> E{MEETS THRESHOLD 5 ppm gluten 2.5 ppm milk}
    E -- Yes --> F[Proceed to Processing]
    E -- No --> G[Re-clean Re-test]
    F --> H[Finished Product]
    H --> I[Label Contains Statement]
    H --> J[May Contain Precautionary Allergen Labelling PAL]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between food allergy and food intolerance. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Food Allergy**: An adverse immune-mediated reaction (typically IgE-mediated Type I hypersensitivity) that occurs reproducibly upon exposure to a specific food protein. The immune system recognises the food as foreign. (1 mark)
- **Food Intolerance**: A non-immunological adverse reaction, usually due to a metabolic deficiency (enzyme, transport, or pharmacological). It does not involve IgE, T-cells, or immune memory. (1 mark)
- **Examples**: Allergy — peanut anaphylaxis, cow's milk urticaria; Intolerance — lactose intolerance (lactase deficiency), favism (G6PD deficiency). (1 mark)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the "Big-8" food allergens. Why is sesame now included in many regulatory lists as a ninth allergen?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Big-8 allergens** (FALCPA, 2004) are: (1) Cow's milk, (2) Egg, (3) Fish, (4) Crustacean shellfish, (5) Tree nuts, (6) Peanuts, (7) Wheat, (8) Soybeans. (2 marks)
- **Sesame** was added as the 9th major allergen by the US FDA in **April 2023** (FASTER Act) because of its increasing prevalence of IgE-mediated allergy (estimated ~ 0.1% global prevalence), severity of reactions (anaphylaxis common), and difficulty in avoiding it (used in tahini, hummus, bakery, oils). (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**
> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]**
> **(a)** Describe in detail the **mechanism of Type I IgE-mediated hypersensitivity** with a neat labelled diagram of the sensitisation and effector phases. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **common allergenic components in foods**, listing at least 8 major food allergens with their characteristic allergenic proteins and a note on threshold doses. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Type I Mechanism (7 marks):**

**Sensitisation Phase (3 marks):**
- Initial exposure to allergen → uptake by gut APCs (dendritic cells of Peyer's patches). (0.5)
- APC processes and presents peptide on **MHC-II** to naïve CD4⁺ T-helper cell. (0.5)
- In presence of **IL-4**, Th0 differentiates into **Th2 cell**, which secretes **IL-4, IL-5, IL-13**. (1.0)
- **IL-4** drives B-cell **class-switch recombination** to produce **allergen-specific IgE** antibodies. (0.5)
- IgE circulates and binds **high-affinity FcεRI receptors** on tissue **mast cells** and blood **basophils** → individual is now *sensitised* (asymptomatic). (0.5)

**Effector Phase (3 marks):**
- Re-exposure to the same allergen → multivalent allergen **cross-links ≥ 2 adjacent IgE** molecules on the mast-cell surface. (0.5)
- This triggers **degranulation**: pre-formed mediators (**histamine, tryptase, heparin**) are released within seconds. (0.5)
- De novo synthesis of **leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4), prostaglandin D2, PAF, and cytokines (TNF-α, IL-4)** within minutes. (0.5)
- These mediators cause: **vasodilation** (hypotension, flushing), **increased vascular permeability** (angioedema), **smooth muscle contraction** (bronchospasm, GI cramps), and **mucus secretion** (rhinorrhoea). (0.5)
- The clinical spectrum ranges from **mild urticaria** to **anaphylactic shock**; a **biphasic reaction** can occur in 5–20% of cases. (0.5)
- **Diagnostic tools**: Skin Prick Test (wheal ≥ 3 mm), Serum Specific IgE (ImmunoCAP), Oral Food Challenge (gold standard). (0.5)

**Diagram (1 mark):** Sensitisation pathway → IgE bound to mast cell → re-exposure → cross-linking → degranulation.

**(b) Allergenic Components in Foods (7 marks):**

| # | Allergen | Key Allergenic Proteins | MW (kDa) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cow's milk | αs1-, αs2-, β-, κ-casein; β-lactoglobulin; α-lactalbumin | 14–25 | Caseins = heat-stable; β-LG survives pasteurisation. **Threshold 0.1 mg** |
| 2 | Hen's egg | Ovomucoid (Gal d 1), Ovalbumin (Gal d 2), Ovotransferrin, Lysozyme | 28–76 | Ovomucoid is heat- and digestion-stable → marker allergen. **0.1 mg** |
| 3 | Fish | Parvalbumin (Gad c 1) | 12 | Ca²⁺-binding, **highly heat-stable**, pan-allergen across species. **0.01–1 mg** |
| 4 | Crustaceans | Tropomyosin (Pen a 1) | 36 | Pan-allergen → cross-reactivity with mites, insects. **0.1–1 mg** |
| 5 | Peanuts | Ara h 1, 2, 3, 6 (storage proteins), Ara h 8 (PR-10) | 16–64 | Ara h 2 most potent; roasting *increases* allergenicity. **0.1 mg** |
| 6 | Tree nuts | 2S albumins, 7S/11S globulins, LTP | 12–60 | Walnut, almond, cashew, hazelnut; cross-reactivity common. **0.01–1 mg** |
| 7 | Wheat | Gliadins, Glutenins, ω-5 gliadin (Tri a 19) | 28–70 | Cause both IgE allergy AND Type IV coeliac disease. **0.1 mg** |
| 8 | Soy | Gly m 5, Gly m 6 (11S globulin), Gly m 8 (2S albumin) | 14–70 | Cross-reactivity with peanut via 2S albumins. **0.1 mg** |

**Threshold Dose Note (1 mark):** VITAL 2.0 reference doses range from **0.01 mg (fish)** to **1.0 mg (peanut, milk)**. Below these, ≤ 1% of the allergic population is expected to react.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE:**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> **(a)** Explain the **pathogenesis of Coeliac disease** with a note on its diagnosis and dietary management. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss **lactose intolerance, phenylketonuria (PKU), and favism** as examples of food disorders associated with metabolism. Highlight the defective enzyme, trigger food, and dietary management of each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Coeliac Disease Pathogenesis (7 marks):**

- **Definition** (0.5): Coeliac disease (gluten-sensitive enteropathy) is a chronic, immune-mediated systemic disorder triggered by **gliadin** (a prolamin fraction of gluten) in genetically susceptible individuals carrying **HLA-DQ2 (~ 95% of patients) or HLA-DQ8 (~ 5%)** haplotypes.
- **Mechanism** (3.5):
  1. Gluten proteins from wheat, barley, rye resist gastric/pancreatic digestion; large immunogenic peptides (33-mer from α-gliadin) reach the small-intestinal lamina propria. (0.5)
  2. **Tissue transglutaminase 2 (tTG2)**, released during intestinal injury, deamidates specific glutamine residues to negatively charged glutamate. (0.5)
  3. Deamidated gliadin binds with high affinity to HLA-DQ2/8 on APCs. (0.5)
  4. **CD4⁺ Th1** cells are activated, releasing **IFN-γ and TNF-α** → chronic inflammation, crypt hyperplasia. (0.5)
  5. **CD8⁺ intraepithelial lymphocytes (IELs)** kill enterocytes via NKG2D-MICA interaction → **villous atrophy**. (0.5)
  6. Also Type III component: anti-tTG IgA autoantibodies form immune complexes. (0.5)
- **Clinical features** (1.0): Chronic diarrhoea, steatorrhoea, weight loss, iron/folate/B12 deficiency, osteopenia, dermatitis herpetiformis (skin), infertility.
- **Diagnosis** (1.0):
  1. Serology: **Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA (anti-tTG IgA)** — first-line, > 95% sensitivity; confirmed with **anti-endomysial IgA (EMA)**.
  2. **Duodenal biopsy** — gold standard; **Marsh grading** (Type 0 normal → Type 3c total villous atrophy).
  3. **HLA-DQ2/DQ8 typing** — useful negative predictor.
  4. **Gluten challenge** (≥ 2 slices of bread/day for 6 weeks) for equivocal cases.
- **Dietary Management** (1.0): **Strict, lifelong gluten-free diet** (GFD) avoiding wheat, rye, barley, and contaminated oats. Allowed: rice, maize, millets, pseudo-cereals (quinoa, amaranth, buckwheat), and certified gluten-free products (< 20 ppm gluten — Codex standard).

**(b) Metabolic Food Disorders (7 marks):**

| Disorder | Defective Enzyme | Trigger Food | Dietary Management | Prevalence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lactose Intolerance** | **Lactase (LCT gene, brush border of jejunum)** | Milk, cheese, ice-cream, dairy | Lactose-restricted diet; fermented dairy (yoghurt often tolerated); lactase enzyme supplements; ≤ 12 g lactose/day usually tolerated | **65–70% adults globally** |
| **Phenylketonuria (PKU)** | **Phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH)** — converts Phe → Tyr (requires BH₄ cofactor) | Protein foods (meat, fish, eggs, dairy, aspartame sweetener) | **Phe-restricted diet** (Phe < 500 mg/day); Tyr-supplemented; **avoid aspartame** (contains Phe); special PKU formulas (e.g., Phenyl-Free) | **1 : 10,000–15,000** |
| **Favism (G6PD Deficiency)** | **Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase** in RBCs (HMP shunt) | **Fava beans** (Vicia faba), also broad beans, certain drugs (primaquine, sulpha drugs) | **Strict avoidance of fava beans** and oxidant drugs; consume other legumes; *no specific enzyme replacement* | **~ 400 million** carriers worldwide |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Type I and Type IV in Coeliac** — students often wrongly classify coeliac as IgE-mediated (Type I). It is a **Type IV (cell-mediated, T-cell) hypersensitivity** with secondary Type III (immune complex) features. Losing **1 mark minimum**.
> 2. **Confusing food allergy with food intolerance** in Q1 — examiners *explicitly* check for the immunological vs. non-immunological distinction. State IgE / T-cell involvement for allergy, enzyme/metabolic cause for intolerance.
> 3. **Forgetting threshold units (mg protein, not mg food)** in numerical questions — KTU boards award the unit mark separately.
> 4. **Not listing the Big-8 in order** — they expect FALCPA-order listing for full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the role of tTG2 in coeliac** — a frequent omission. tTG2-mediated deamidation is the *rate-limiting immunological step* and is worth **0.5–1 mark** in part (a).
> 6. **Missing aspartame in PKU** — phenylketonuria literally means *"phenyl in the urine"*; aspartame contains ~ 50% Phe. Forgetting this loses a critical mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Quick-Recall — Read 2× before exam day.**

- [ ] **Hypersensitivity** = reproducible immune reaction; **Intolerance** = non-immune metabolic.
- [ ] **Coombs & Gell 4 types**: I (IgE, immediate), II (cytotoxic), III (immune complex), IV (T-cell, delayed).
- [ ] **Type I is the "anaphylaxis" type** — IgE + mast cells + histamine/leukotrienes/PAF.
- [ ] **Type I Sensitisation**: Allergen → APC → Th2 (IL-4) → B-cell class switch → IgE → FcεRI on mast cells.
- [ ] **Type I Effector**: Re-exposure → cross-linking → **degranulation** → 4 clinical pillars (Skin, GI, Resp, CV).
- [ ] **Coeliac Disease**: Type IV (T-cell), triggered by **gliadin**, requires **HLA-DQ2/DQ8**, mediated by **tTG2 deamidation**, diagnosed by **anti-tTG IgA + biopsy (Marsh grade)**, treated by **strict gluten-free diet (< 20 ppm)**.
- [ ] **Big-8 / Big-9 allergens** (FALCPA + sesame): milk, egg, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soy [+ sesame].
- [ ] **Pan-allergens** (cross-reactivity): **Tropomyosin** (crustaceans ↔ mites), **Parvalbumin** (fish), **2S albumins** (peanut/soy/tree nuts), **PR-10** (OAS with birch pollen).
- [ ] **Lactose Intolerance**: lactase ↓; H₂ breath test (+ve if Δ ≥ 20 ppm); yoghurt often tolerated.
- [ ] **PKU**: PAH deficiency; **Phe-restricted diet + avoid aspartame**.
- [ ] **Favism**: G6PD⁻; avoid fava beans + oxidant drugs (primaquine, sulpha, naphthalene).
- [ ] **Galactosaemia**: GALT deficiency; **galactose-free** soy-based formula in infancy.
- [ ] **Scombroid poisoning**: histamine ≥ 50 mg/100 g; *pseudo-allergy*; *no immune memory*.
- [ ] **Threshold doses (VITAL 2.0)**: range 0.01 – 1.0 mg total protein; protect 99% of allergic population.
- [ ] **Allergen characteristics**: typically 10–70 kDa glycoproteins, acid- and heat-stable (true allergens), or heat-labile (OAS).
- [ ] **Cooking effects**: Roasted peanuts → *↑* Ara h 2; baked milk → *↓* IgE binding; boiled milk → tolerance induction.
- [ ] **Diagnostic gold standard**: Double-Blind Placebo-Controlled Food Challenge (DBPCFC).
- [ ] **Component-Resolved Diagnostics (CRD)**: distinguishes true sensitisation from cross-reactivity (e.g., Ara h 2 vs Ara h 8 in peanut).
- [ ] **ELISA detection limits** (industry): < 2.5 ppm milk, < 5 ppm gluten, < 5 ppm soy.
- [ ] **Indian regulatory context**: FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006) mandates allergen labelling under the 2020 Food Safety and Standards (Labelling and Display) Regulations; aligns with Codex.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — PEFTT634 Food Toxicology**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lactose Intolerance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT634 - FOOD TOXICOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lactose Intolerance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview
